entry.title: IS-U to S/4HANA utilities - Architecture in LeanIX
entry.content:
Hello, 
would anyone, please, share a draw.io/LeanIX SAP LeanIX solutions # architecture model for all modules/scope items of IS-U and S/4HANA utilities? This could help to model it for other architects, instead of reinventing the wheel 🙂
I have been following the SAP LeanIX modeling guidelines for S/4HANA systems and ECC systems and modeled ISU and S/4HANA utilities as L1 applications, and the Modules/enabled Scope Items as L2 applications and assigned business capabilities (BCs) to L2 applications, however I would like to be sure about all module items/scope items in both solutions and BCs assigned...

I am sharing links to LeanIX modeling guidelines for ECC:
Modeling SAP ERP 6.0 | SAP Help Portal
and S/4HANA:Modeling SAP S/4HANA | SAP Help Portal


thanks
-Ivo

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite - Operations - Cloud Foundry runtime and service keys
entry.content:
Dear Community,

this blog provides practical insights into structuring Service Keys for SAP Integration Suite, a topic that is often
overlooked during initial setup but becomes critical as landscapes grow.

Intention

With the move to Integration Suite as middleware, customers quickly reach a point where they need to govern access to
their Integrations properly. Who is allowed to trigger which scenario? How are credentials managed, rotated, and
audited? SAP provides many resources to help with the broader landscape setup but the specifics of Service Instance
and Service Key design are rarely addressed in detail.

Approach

We focus on one specific design decision as best practices: how to name and structure Service Instances and Service Keys
for the Process Integration Runtime (pir) in Integration Suite as well as how to protect Integration Flow endpoints using Custom
Sender Roles. Based on multiple customer ramp-ups, a clear and repeatable structure has emerged that scales well and supports both modern and legacy authentication requirements.

Solution

The Problem with unstructured Service Keys:

  • Shared Service Keys used by multiple systems - one key, no traceability
  • Basic Auth credentials shared across scenarios - a single compromised key impacts everything
  • No separation between sender systems or business processes
  • Missing Custom Roles - every key can trigger every iFlow

These patterns make auditing difficult, key rotation risky, and access control nearly impossible to enforce cleanly.

Naming Convention - Service Instances

source: Create a Service Instance in SAP BTP (Dev Tutorial) ,  SAP Help - Service Instances in Cloud Foundry;

Our recommended basic structure for a Service Instance is:

  • pir_{type}_{sendersystem}_{project/scenario}
SegmentDescription Examples
typeRuntime typeflow, api
sendersystemSource system or platform which connects to Integration Suitep01, h01, advancedeventmesh, calm, n8n
project/scenarioBusiness process or project - omit if genericmaterialmaster, payroll, monitoring

Examples: 

pir_flow_p01
pir_flow_p01_materialmaster
pir_flow_advancedeventmesh
pir_api_calm_monitoring
pir_api_integrationsuite
pir_flow_h01_payroll
pir_flow_h01_elster

Important: When a Custom Role is required, create a dedicated Service Instance and Service Key per Custom Role:

  • pir_{type}_{sendersystem}_{project/scenario}_{custom_role}

This ensures that each Custom Role maps exactly to one instance and one dedicated key — no shared access, clean
separation.

Naming Convention - Service Keys

OAuth2 / Basic Auth:
sk_{project/scenario}

Good to know: A Service Key for the process-integration plan provisions both Basic Auth and OAuth2 (Client Credentials) credentials within the same key. This supports backwards compatibility: legacy systems use Basic Auth while modern systems use OAuth2 with the same key, without maintaining parallel credentials.

Examples:

sk_z_report_materialmaster
sk_h01
sk_eventenablement
sk_showcase_salesorder

Certificate-based Authentication - add _c_ after the prefix:

  • sk_c_{project/scenario}

Examples:

sk_c_userexchange_saml2
sk_c_payroll
sk_c_elster

Full Example:

Without Custom Role - one instance, but a dedicated Service Key per scenario to maintain traceability:

Instancepir_flow_p01 
Keysk_p01generic, for simple/shared access e.g. in DEV
Keysk_z_report_materialmasterdedicated, for Z-Report scenario
Keysk_eventenablementdedicated, e.g. for Event Enablement project

Hint: sharing a single key across multiple scenarios defeats the purpose of auditability.

With Custom Role - each Custom Role requires its own dedicated instance and key:

Example 1:

Instancepir_flow_h01_payrollholds this role: PayrollSender
iFlowPayrollSync_H01Integration Flow in Integration Suite
RolePayrollSenderCustom Role assigned to iFlow endpoint
Keysk_c_payrolldedicated key, with certificate

Example 2:

Instancepir_flow_h01_elsterholds this role: ElsterSender
iFlowElsterSubmit_H01_customIntegration Flow in Integration Suite
RoleElsterSenderCustom Role assigned to iFlow endpoint
Keysk_c_elsterdedicated key, with certificate

Hint: A generic key such as sk_h01 would not hold the Custom Role and therefore cannot trigger the protected iFlow - the
dedicated key is mandatory.

When to Create a Dedicated Instance?

SituationDedicated Instance
Different sender systemYes
Sensitive process (Payroll, Elster, HR)Yes
Custom Role requiredYes - one instance and one key per Custom Role
Generic shared flows, no role restriction neededNo - one instance but multiple dedicated keys

Conclusion

This design gives you:

  • Clean audit trails - every call traceable to a specific key and system
  • Safe key rotation - rotating one key affects exactly one scenario or system
  • Controlled access - Custom Role ensures only authorized keys trigger the Integration Flow

A consistent naming convention and the deliberate use of Custom Sender Roles are small investments with significant
operational returns. They give your Integration Suite landscape the auditability and security posture that enterprise
middleware requires - and the dual Basic Auth / OAuth2 support within a single Service Key means you can implement
this without forcing every connected system to upgrade at once.

Feel free to use the comment section or reach out to us via SAP Integration Services.

Modernize. Integrate. Innovate.

entry.title: Re: F110 - Payment Proposal Workflow error
entry.content:
Hi All,
I was facing a similar issue, but in my case, the workflow was remaining at the wait task when workflow was executed even without any changes to standard workflow. Therefore, I did not have any previous working version of the workflow to revert to.
I resolved the issue by executing the standard report RSWWCOND using a predefined variant, which completed the workflow successfully.

Hope this helps!

entry.title: Re: Want some of tolerance in the CO11N after Production...
entry.content: In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, the standard CO11N confirmation process does indeed allow users to manually adjust component consumption quantities without restriction. To enforce tolerance limits, you will need to introduce a control mechanism via In‑App Extensibility (Custom Logic) plus Configurable Tolerance Field → ensures users cannot post unrealistic component consumption, while still allowing controlled flexibility.

entry.title: Re: Low value asset area 01 not maintained
entry.content:
If you are utilizing the low-value asset solution, it is also necessary to define the corresponding configuration settings.

To assist you in understanding the functionality and configuration options for low-value assets, please refer to the following documentation:

Low-Value Assets (LVA)

367931 - Amount check low-value assets

entry.title: CRUD Operations with Image Upload using SAPUI5, Node.js, Express, MySQL, and Multer
entry.content:
Hi, 
 
Introduction: 

Media such as images, videos, and icons play an important role in making applications visually appealing and interactive, which helps improve user engagement. 

In this blog, we will learn how to perform CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) operations with image upload functionality using: 

  • SAPUI5 for the frontend  
  • Node.js and Express.js for the backend  
  • MySQL as the database  
  • Multer for handling file uploads 

Backend Implementation using Node.js

Prerequisite - Install MySQL Workbench/MySQL Server and create a database. 

 
Step 1 : Create a backend folder and inside it create a file named index.js 
And run the following commands  

  • npm init –y 
  • npm i express cors mysql2 multer 

 

Command 

Purpose 

npm init -y 

Initializes a Node.js project with default configurations 

npm i express 

Installs Express.js framework for backend APIs 

npm i cors 

Enables Cross-Origin Resource Sharing between frontend and backend 

npm i mysql2 

Connects Node.js application with MySQL database 

npm i multer 

Handles file uploads 

 
Step 2 : Create the uploads folder 

Note: Make sure the uploads folder exists before starting the server. 
Otherwise, multer may throw an error while uploading files. 

 
Step 3 : Add the below code in index.js 

Note : Enter the MySQL password (the one you created during MySQL Workbench/MySQL server Installation)

index.js file

const express = require("express")
const cors = require("cors")
const mysql = require("mysql2")
const multer = require("multer")

const app = express()

app.use(express.json())
app.use(cors())

// Database Connection
const db = mysql.createConnection({
    host: 'localhost',
    user: 'root',
    password: 'Root@1234',
    database: 'student_db'
})

db.connect((err) => {
    if (err) {
        console.log("Database Connection Failed")
        console.log(err.message)
    } else {
        console.log("Database Connected Successfully")
    }
})

// Create Table
db.query(`CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS users(
id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
name VARCHAR(50),
city VARCHAR(50),
image VARCHAR(255)
)`, (err) => {
    if (err) {
        console.log("Table Creation Failed")
    } else {
        console.log("Users Table Ready")
    }
})


// Multer Storage
const storage = multer.diskStorage({
    destination: (req, file, cb) => {
        cb(null, "uploads")
    },
    filename: (req, file, cb) => {
        cb(null, Date.now() + "-" + file.originalname)
    }
})

const upload = multer({
    storage: storage
})


// Insert User
app.post("/users", upload.single("image"), (req, res) => {

    let name = req.body.name
    let city = req.body.city
    let image = req.file.filename

    db.query(
        "INSERT INTO users(name,city,image) VALUES(?,?,?)",
        [name, city, image],
        (err) => {
            if (err) {
                console.log("Failed to Insert User")
                return res.send(err)
            }

            console.log(`User Inserted : ${name}`)
            res.send("Inserted")
        }
    )
})


// Get Users
app.get("/users", (req, res) => {

    db.query("SELECT * FROM users", (err, result) => {

        if (err) {
            console.log("Failed to Fetch Users")
            return res.send(err)
        }

        console.log("Users Data Fetched")
        res.send(result)
    })
})


// Update User
app.put("/users/:id", upload.single("image"), (req, res) => {

    let id = req.params.id
    let name = req.body.name
    let city = req.body.city

    if (req.file) {

        let image = req.file.filename

        db.query(
            "UPDATE users SET name=?,city=?,image=? WHERE id=?",
            [name, city, image, id],
            (err) => {

                if (err) {
                    console.log(`Failed to Update User ID : ${id}`)
                    return res.send(err)
                }

                console.log(`User Updated With Image : ID ${id}`)
                res.send("Updated")
            }
        )

    } else {

        db.query(
            "UPDATE users SET name=?,city=? WHERE id=?",
            [name, city, id],
            (err) => {

                if (err) {
                    console.log(`Failed to Update User ID : ${id}`)
                    return res.send(err)
                }

                console.log(`User Updated : ID ${id}`)
                res.send("Updated")
            }
        )
    }
})


// Delete User
app.delete("/users/:id", (req, res) => {

    let id = req.params.id

    db.query(
        "DELETE FROM users WHERE id=?",
        [id],
        (err) => {

            if (err) {
                console.log(`Failed to Delete User ID : ${id}`)
                return res.send(err)
            }

            console.log(`User Deleted : ID ${id}`)
            res.send("Deleted")
        }
    )
})


// Static Folder
app.use("/uploads", express.static("uploads"))


// Server Start
app.listen(3000, () => {
    console.log("Server Running Successfully")
    console.log("URL : http://localhost:3000")
})

Code explanation - Initially, all the required packages are imported. Then a connection to MySQL is established, and an uploads folder is used to store uploaded images. 

Here, the database name is student_db. After verifying the database connection, the application creates a table named users table inside the database (if table does not exists). 

Multer stores uploaded files on the server disk. All uploaded images are saved inside the uploads folder. To avoid duplicate file names, the uploaded image name is prefixed with the current timestamp using Date.now(). 

 
const upload = multer({ 
    storage: storage 
}) 

This connects multer with storage. 

upload.single("image") 

This middleware is used to upload a single file. 

  • "image" represents the field name  
  • The frontend or Postman form-data key must also be named image 

Then create API calls for Create, Read, Update and Delete 

app.use("/uploads", express.static("uploads")) 

This line exposes the uploads folder as a static directory so that uploaded images can be accessed directly through a URL. 

Example: 

uploads/17434img.png 

Access via: 

http://localhost:3000/uploads/17434img.png 

The listen() method starts the server. 
The first parameter represents the port number on which the application runs. 

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 

Frontend Implementation using SAPUI5

Create the SAPUI5 application using basic template. 

In the view, create a form that accepts: 

  • Name  
  • City  
  • Image upload 

To upload images in SAPUI5, we use: 

  • Library: sap.ui.unified  
  • Control: FileUploader 

Three buttons are created for performing below operations: 

  • Create  
  • Update  
  • Delete  

 
After submitting the form, the user data is displayed in a list along with: 

  • User image  
  • Name  
  • City 

This is View1.view.xml file

<mvc:View controllerName="project1.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns:u="sap.ui.unified"
    xmlns="sap.m">
    <Page id="page" title="{i18n>title}">
        <VBox>
            <Input id="name" placeholder="Enter Name" />
            <Input id="city" placeholder="Enter City" />

            <u:FileUploader id="fileUploader" name="image" change="onFileSelect" />

            <Button text="Create" press="onCreate" />
            <Button text="Update" press="onUpdate" />
            <Button text="Delete" press="onDelete" />
            
            <List id="userList" />
            <Image id="preview" width="150px" />
        </VBox>
    </Page>
</mvc:View>

View Explanation 

  • Input controls are used for entering user details.  
  • FileUploader is used to select images.  
  • Button controls trigger CRUD operations.  
  • List displays all user records.  
  • Image control shows image preview before upload. 


This is View1.controller.js file

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller",
    "sap/m/MessageToast",         
    "sap/m/StandardListItem"      
], (Controller, MessageToast, StandardListItem) => {
    "use strict";

    return Controller.extend("project1.controller.View1", {

        onInit() {
            this.loadData();
        },

        loadData: function () {
            fetch("http://localhost:3000/users")
                .then(res => res.json())
                .then(data => {
                    let list = this.byId("userList");
                    list.removeAllItems();

                    data.forEach(user => {
                        list.addItem(
                            new StandardListItem({  
                                title: user.name,
                                description: user.city,
                                icon: "http://localhost:3000/uploads/" + user.image,
                                type: "Active",
                                press: () => {
                                    this.selectedId = user.id;
                                    this.byId("name").setValue(user.name);
                                    this.byId("city").setValue(user.city);
                                }
                            })
                        );
                    });
                });
        },

        onFileSelect: function (e) {
            let file = e.getParameter("files")[0];
            this.selectedFile = file;

            let reader = new FileReader();
            reader.onload = (evt) => {
                this.byId("preview").setSrc(evt.target.result);
            };
            reader.readAsDataURL(file);
        },

        onCreate: function () {
            let name = this.byId("name").getValue();
            let city = this.byId("city").getValue();

            let formData = new FormData();
            formData.append("name", name);
            formData.append("city", city);
            formData.append("image", this.selectedFile);

            fetch("http://localhost:3000/users", {
                method: "POST",
                body: formData
            }).then(() => {
                MessageToast.show("Created");  
                this.loadData();
            });
        },

        onUpdate: function () {
            let name = this.byId("name").getValue();
            let city = this.byId("city").getValue();

            let formData = new FormData();
            formData.append("name", name);
            formData.append("city", city);

            if (this.selectedFile)
                formData.append("image", this.selectedFile);

            fetch("http://localhost:3000/users/" + this.selectedId, {
                method: "PUT",
                body: formData
            }).then(() => {
                MessageToast.show("Updated");  
                this.loadData();
            });
        },

        onDelete: function () {
            fetch("http://localhost:3000/users/" + this.selectedId, {
                method: "DELETE"
            }).then(() => {
                MessageToast.show("Deleted");  
                this.loadData();
            });
        }
    });
});

Controller Logic Explanation :

  • onInit() loads existing user data when the application starts.  
  • loadData() fetches all records from the backend and displays them in the SAPUI5 list.  
  • onFileSelect() reads the selected image and displays a preview.  
  • onCreate() sends form data to the backend using a POST request.  
  • onUpdate() updates existing user details using a PUT request.  
  • onDelete() removes a user record using a DELETE request. 

 

Conclusion : In this blog, we implemented CRUD operations with image handling using SAPUI5, Node.js, Express.js, MySQL, and Multer. 

The application allows users to: 

  • Upload images  
  • Store user information in MySQL  
  • Retrieve and display images dynamically  
  • Update existing records  
  • Delete records efficiently  

Multer is used for handling file uploads, while Express APIs manage backend operations. SAPUI5 provides a simple and interactive frontend for managing user data and displaying uploaded images.

entry.title: Re: How to get and stay Certified in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, Asset Management ?
entry.content: A well-structured certification path combined with continuous assessment helps maintain high-quality implementation expertise across the SAP ecosystem.

entry.title: Re: AI Needs Clean Data. Business Networks Stop It Getting Dirty in the First Place
entry.content:
Excellent perspective on AI readiness and the importance of trusted data foundations. The comparison between “cleaning dirty data” and preventing bad data from entering in the first place is especially relevant for modern supply chains.

The concept of “Golden Inputs” is a practical way to approach agentic AI adoption without waiting for a perfect enterprise-wide data cleanup initiative. SAP Business Network clearly provides strong value here by enabling structured, validated, and trustworthy data flows across trading partners.

A very insightful read connecting AI, governance, and operational reality in a way enterprises can actually apply.

entry.title: HR Transformation for Line Managers: A Six-Month Enablement Framework
entry.content:
From Task Broker to People Process Owner

Many organizations expect line managers to take on a stronger role in HR processes, but still enable them as occasional users of a system. That is not enough. A manager who understands where to click may still be unable to perform the role the new operating model requires. This article is the second of two closely related publications. The first article explained why line managers can become either a pebble in the shoe or a game changer in HR transformation. This second part outlines a practical six-month framework to help them move from task brokers to People Process Owners.

Why a framework is needed

Developing line managers into effective People Process Owners does not happen through a single training event. It requires a structured enablement journey that combines role clarity, process understanding, guided practice, feedback, and performance transparency.
The starting point is often modest. Many managers are limited process participants today. They forward requests, approve tasks, escalate issues, or ask HR to resolve operational questions. Over six months, the goal should be to help them move from brokering HR tasks to actively owning the people processes that sit within their leadership role.

A six-month enablement framework

Month 1: Establish role clarity

The first step is to make the future role explicit. Managers need to understand what changes, which responsibilities move closer to the business, and where HR will continue to support them.
Recommended competencies:
  • Understand the target HR operating model and the manager's role within it.
  • Distinguish between HR-owned, shared, and manager-owned responsibilities.
  • Recognize the manager's impact on employee experience, data quality, and process performance.

Month 2: Build process literacy

Managers need to understand end-to-end processes, not just individual system steps. This includes what triggers a process, which decisions are required, what good input looks like, and what happens downstream.
Recommended competencies:
  • Explain the main steps of core people processes such as hiring, onboarding, performance, development, job changes, absence, and offboarding.
  • Understand dependencies between manager actions, HR Operations, HR Consultancy, shared services, and employees.
  • Identify common failure points caused by delayed, incomplete, or poor-quality manager input.

Month 3: Strengthen decision and data ownership

Once managers understand the process, they need to become more confident in the decisions and data they own. This is where the role starts to shift from passive participation to active ownership.
Recommended competencies:
  • Make people-related decisions within defined policy and governance boundaries.
  • Provide complete, timely, and accurate input at the source.
  • Understand how manager-owned data affects reporting, compliance, automation, AI, and workforce decisions.

Month 4: Practice employee experience ownership

Managers need to connect their process role to employee moments of truth. The focus should move beyond task completion toward the quality of the employee experience.
Recommended competencies:
  • Lead key employee interactions linked to onboarding, performance, feedback, development, compensation, mobility, and offboarding.
  • Communicate process steps and expectations clearly to employees.
  • Recognize when employee experience issues require HR Consultancy support.

Month 5: Adopt digital and AI-enabled working

At this stage, managers should become more effective users of digital workflows, analytics, recommendations, and AI-supported guidance. The emphasis should be on responsible use: technology supports the manager, but does not replace judgment or accountability.
Recommended competencies:
  • Use guided workflows confidently and consistently.
  • Interpret basic HR dashboards and process indicators.
  • Use AI-supported recommendations or prompts as decision support, not as a substitute for leadership judgment.

Month 6: Embed performance and continuous improvement

The final month should focus on embedding the role into normal management routines. Managers should be able to monitor their own process performance, understand where they need support, and contribute to improving the operating model over time.
Recommended competencies:
  • Review own process performance using simple KPIs.
  • Identify recurring issues in one's own area of responsibility and take corrective action.
  • Provide structured feedback to HR on process usability, policy clarity, and enablement needs.

Simple KPIs for measuring People Process Owner performance

The KPI set should be easy to implement, practical for customers, and focused on behaviors managers can actually influence. It should not become overly complex or punitive. The purpose is to create transparency, guide support, and reinforce the new role.

KPI

What it measures

Why it matters

On-time manager task completion

Percentage of manager-owned HR process tasks completed within the expected timeframe.

Shows whether managers are actively fulfilling their role and not creating downstream delays.

First-time-right input

Percentage of manager-submitted requests or data entries accepted without HR rework.

Indicates whether managers understand the required quality of their input.

HR rework / clarification rate

Number or percentage of manager actions requiring HR correction, follow-up, or clarification.

Highlights where managers need better guidance, coaching, or process enablement.

Self-service usage rate

Percentage of relevant manager-owned actions completed through self-service rather than HR intervention.

Shows whether responsibilities are truly shifting into the business.

Employee moment completion

Completion of key manager-owned moments, such as onboarding check-ins, performance reviews, feedback conversations, and development discussions.

Links process ownership directly to employee experience.

Manager-owned data quality

Accuracy and completeness of data elements created, approved, or validated by managers.

Reinforces data ownership at source and supports reporting, automation, and AI.

Manager process confidence

Simple pulse score asking managers how confident they feel executing key people processes.

Identifies capability gaps that system usage data alone may not reveal.

Employee experience feedback

Employee feedback on manager-supported people moments.

Measures whether processes are experienced as clear, timely, and useful.

These KPIs should be used to support the new role, not to punish managers for every process issue. HR Consultancy roles, such as HR Business Partners, should use the indicators to identify where managers need coaching, clarification, or targeted enablement. They should help when needed, but they should not reclaim operative work that belongs in the business.

Conclusion

A modern HR operating model requires line managers to move beyond brokering tasks. They need role clarity, process literacy, decision confidence, data ownership, employee experience awareness, and the ability to work with digital and AI-enabled tools. A six-month enablement framework gives organizations a practical path to build these capabilities. The objective is not to turn managers into HR administrators. It is to make the people-related part of leadership visible, measurable, and supportable.

entry.title: HR Transformation for Line Managers: Are We Replacing Systems or Ways of Working?
entry.content:
Are we replacing systems, or replacing ways of working?

In short, we should be doing both.

An HR transformation is not primarily about replacing legacy systems. It is a response to changing business expectations, persistent HR delivery challenges, and new technological possibilities.

Many transformation journeys start from a traditional HR operating model, where processes, responsibilities, and systems have grown around historical ways of working. In these environments, HR often compensates for unclear ownership, fragmented processes, local variation, and limited self-service through manual intervention and expert-driven execution. When this model is simply transferred into a modern cloud solution, the organization risks modernizing the technology while preserving the limitations of the old operating model.

Modern HR platforms such as SAP SuccessFactors HCM are designed around a different logic. They support standardized and harmonized processes, guided employee and manager experiences, self-service, shared services, clearer role ownership, data input at source, global workflows, continuous innovation, and increasingly, embedded AI.

The value of such a transformation depends less on the system itself and more on the organization's ability to adopt the operating model that the system enables. This requires a shift in roles, behaviors, accountabilities, and expectations across HR, employees, and the business.

Line Managers: The Pebble in Your Shoe, or Game Changer?

One of the most affected stakeholders in an HR transformation is the line manager. In a traditional operating model, line managers may have acted mainly as requesters, approvers, or escalation points. In a modern cloud-based HR operating model, their role changes materially. They become active owners of key people-related moments and decisions.

This makes the line manager either a game changer or a pebble in your shoe. Small enough to be underestimated, but disruptive enough to slow every step of the journey.

This article is not about explaining the general virtues of change management. Communication plans, stakeholder engagement, training schedules, milestone updates, and adoption campaigns all matter. But even when these elements are in place, HR transformations often fall short in one specific area: process enablement.

Understanding why the transformation is happening does not mean line managers know how to contribute to the new process. Understanding how the software works does not mean they understand their role in making the process effective. Access to digital workflows, AI-enabled guidance, and self-service tools does not automatically create ownership.

Three ways line managers can create friction

Communication: When managers do not carry the message.

Line managers are often the most immediate and trusted point of contact for employees. If they cannot explain what is changing, why it matters, and what employees should do differently, the transformation narrative breaks down at execution. Employees continue to ask HR for answers that should be handled by managers. Managers redirect questions back to HR because they are unsure of their own role. The new model is formally live, but the old dependency pattern remains intact.

Enablement and empowerment: When access does not equal capability.

Modern HR platforms give line managers more direct responsibility. They may initiate changes, validate data, approve requests, provide feedback, support talent decisions, trigger development actions, or manage employee moments that were previously handled by HR.

But empowerment is not created by system access alone. A manager who knows which button to click may still not understand the business meaning of the decision, the expected quality standard, the policy boundary, or the downstream impact of poor input. AI and guided experiences can support the manager, but they do not remove the need for accountability.

Personal value: When managers do not see why it matters to them.

Line managers are under pressure. They manage delivery, performance, people issues, priorities, escalations, and business outcomes. If the new HR model is perceived as simply shifting administrative work from HR to the business, resistance is predictable.

For managers to become active contributors, they need to understand the personal value of the change. Better data can help them lead their teams. Faster processes can reduce their own workload. Clearer responsibilities can protect them from ambiguity. Performance, talent, and development processes can help them build stronger teams. Without this connection, the transformation remains an HR initiative. With it, the line manager can become a game changer.

From Process Participant to People Process Owner: Future Capabilities for Line Managers

If the role of the line manager changes, the required capabilities must change as well. Traditional system training is not enough. Future-ready managers need to understand how their actions contribute to end-to-end HR processes, employee experience, data quality, compliance, and business outcomes. They do not need to become HR experts, but they do need to become capable owners of the people-related responsibilities that sit within their leadership role.

Process literacy: Line managers need to understand the end-to-end HR process, not only their individual task in the system. They should know what happens before and after their action, which dependencies exist, and how delays, poor input, or unclear decisions affect the wider process.

Decision accountability: Managers need clarity on which people-related decisions they own, which decisions require HR support, and where policy or governance boundaries apply. This includes understanding the quality standard expected from their input and the consequences of incomplete or inconsistent decisions.

Data ownership: In a modern HR model, data quality starts at the source. Line managers must understand that the data they create, validate, or approve affects reporting, compliance, automation, AI-enabled recommendations, workforce planning, and employee experience.

Employee experience ownership: Managers play a decisive role in key moments of truth, such as onboarding, feedback, performance, development, mobility, absence, and offboarding. The employee experience is not created by the HR system alone; it is shaped by how managers use the process and engage with employees.

Digital and AI-enabled working: Managers need to use guided workflows, analytics, recommendations, and AI-supported interactions effectively and responsibly. Digital tools can simplify decisions, surface insights, and reduce administrative effort, but they do not remove managerial accountability.

The core message is simple: future-ready line managers do not need to become HR specialists, but they do need to become competent owners of the people processes that are part of their leadership role. HR Consultancy roles, such as HR Business Partners, remain essential. Their role is to advise, coach, and support managers when needed - not to take back operative responsibilities that should sit with the business.

Conclusion

HR transformation succeeds when the operating model changes with the technology. Modern cloud platforms, self-service, shared services, automation, and AI can make the new model easier to execute, but they cannot create ownership on their own. Line managers are central to this shift. If they remain passive task brokers, they become a persistent source of friction. If they are enabled as people process owners, they become one of the most powerful levers for adoption, data quality, employee experience, and business impact.

entry.title: SAP SOLMAN E-signature on status change
entry.content:
SAP SOLMAN – digital Signature
Requirement – Upon every status change for CD/ RFC, a digital signature pop- up should open, once successfully signed, user detail, along with entered comment, changed status should be captured in Text log section.

Here I will provide a step-by-step guide:
To create a new custom component (the screen for digital signature)
T-code: BSP_WD_CMPWB

View needs to be created – View name - POPUP

Now to call this screen from CD/RFC, we have standard components:
AI_CMCD_H / AI_CMCR_H
Created component usage in source component, Component Usage - CUdigisign

Within that, introduced an inbound plug - OPENURL

Then created a inbound plug with same name, i.e. OPENURL, in Z_DIGISIGN view, i.e. POPUP,

then create a navigational link in custom component, i.e. Z_DIGISIGN
Navigational link – openpopup, and consumed the inbound plug of i.e. OPENURL, in targets

Now to write the below code, to open the pop up, in source component, i.e. AI_CMCR_H, if your condition matches, here on matching the condition, I have set a flag (Y), based on which pop will open

Data: lv_popup TYPE REF TO if_bsp_wd_popup.
IF gv_ds_popup = 'Y'.
IF lv_popup IS NOT BOUND.
lv_popup = me->comp_controller->window_manager->create_popup(
iv_interface_view_name = 'Z_DIGISIGN/PopupWindow'
iv_usage_name = 'CUdigisign'
iv_title = 'Digital Signature' ).

lv_popup->set_window_width( iv_width = 600 ).
lv_popup->set_window_height( iv_height = 400 ).
ENDIF.
lv_popup->open( 'OPENURL' ).
lv_digi_sign = zl_z_digisi_popup_impl=>gv_digi_sign.

entry.title: SAP Convergent Mediation by DigitalRoute – SOC 1 Compliance Support
entry.content:
Audit and Compliance

Public

May 2026

Executive Summary

Organizations that process revenue-bearing transaction data, across industries such as telecom, finance, and digital services, operate under increasing scrutiny from auditors to assess controls over financial reporting. When that processing relies on mediation platforms like SAP Convergent Mediation by DigitalRoute, a critical question emerges: how does the platform support the control environment required for SOC 1 compliance?

This post is for control owners, IT auditors, and compliance teams evaluating how SAP Convergent Mediation fits into a SOC 1 (SSAE 18 / ISAE 3402) control framework. It covers the platform's built-in capabilities, what customers must implement independently, and how configuration properties map to confidentiality, integrity, and availability objectives.

Key distinction: DigitalRoute develops and ships SAP Convergent Mediation. Customers deploy, operate, and govern the platform within their own environments. This means SOC 1 control ownership sits with the operating organization. The platform provides capabilities that support those controls, but does not replace the governance, approval workflows, or segregation of duties that auditors expect.

Note: SAP Convergent Mediation is a component in Billing and Revenue Innovation Management (BRIM). Its primary role is to collect revenue-bearing usage and priced transactions from the outside world (operational domain) and feed high quality data into the BRIM financial processes. Hence, the SOC 1 compliancy scope described here covers SAP Convergent Mediation as such, and not the whole of BRIM. 

This blog post was co-authored with Yaad Karim, CISO DigitalRoute.

The Shared Responsibility Model

Before mapping specific controls, it is essential to understand who owns what. SOC 1 auditors will look for clear boundaries between the product vendor and the operating entity.

 

The platform generates evidence. The customer governs the process around that evidence. Auditors assess both.

Mapping to SOC 1 ITGC Control Objectives

SOC 1 reports typically address IT General Controls (ITGCs) across several domains. SAP Convergent Mediation provides relevant capabilities in three of the four core ITGC areas.

 1. Change Management

Control Objective: Ensure that all changes to system configurations are recorded, traceable to an individual, and reviewable for accuracy, completeness, and authorization.

This is typically the most scrutinized ITGC domain in a SOC 1 audit. Auditors want to see that changes are authorized before implementation, that a record exists of what changed, and that changes can be traced back to a specific individual and point in time.

What the platform provides

Capability

What it does

Evidence it produces

System Log

Records user-initiated changes such as user creation, password updates, and login attempts, including timestamp, user identity, and severity metadata

Timestamped, attributed change records

Configuration History Tab

Maintains historical versions of configurations (workflows, agents, profiles) showing what changed, when, and by whom

Version-level change traceability

Configuration Tracer / Diff

Highlights differences between configuration versions, identifying specific changes with attribution

Side-by-side change comparison evidence

System Exporter

Exports selected configurations for external review or pre-release evidence snapshots

Point-in-time configuration baselines

Audit Profiles

Tracks batch workflow behavior and data flow for operational completeness verification

Processing integrity evidence

  System Log with Filter dialog. Events are recorded with timestamp, severity, type, and user attribution. Filters allow targeted evidence extraction by severity, date range, username, and message content.

  Configuration Diff showing side-by-side comparison of two APL code versions. Green highlights (left) show the source version; red highlights (right) show the target. Each version displays the timestamp and username of the change, providing the what/who/when triad for change management evidence.

  Workflow History dialog listing every configuration version with modified date, user, and free-text comment. Each entry captures who update the workflow and when it was updated. Optionally, the system implementor can add a comment to describe the why, aligning the platform's technical traceability with the approval trail in the customer's ITSM system.

What the customer must implement

  • Operate a formal change management process (e.g., change advisory board, ticket-based approvals) in their own ITSM tooling
  • Ensure segregation of duties between change requestors, approvers, and implementers
  • Retain evidence of approval outside the platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira tickets)
  • Periodically reconcile platform change logs against approved change records

Auditor note: The Configuration Tracer/Diff capability is particularly valuable for walkthrough testing. It provides the "what changed" evidence that auditors can match against approved change tickets in the customer's change management system.

2. Logical Access - Sensitive Access Monitoring

Control Objective: Identify and review activities of privileged users to ensure that sensitive access is used appropriately and that access rights remain commensurate with job responsibilities.

Logical access controls are the second pillar auditors examine. For mediation platforms processing financial transaction data, this means demonstrating that administrative access is controlled, monitored, and periodically reviewed.

What the platform provides

Capability

What it does

Evidence it produces

System Log

Records administrator activities: user creation, group assignment, password changes, login events. Supports filtering by username and event type

Privileged activity audit trail

Access Controller

Manages role-based privileges through Access Groups. Write permissions required for system changes

Role-to-permission mapping

Login Security (Advanced Tab)

Tracks failed login attempts, account lockouts, and optionally logs successful logins

Authentication event records

What the customer must implement

  • Define and enforce a formal access provisioning and deprovisioning process
  • Conduct periodic access reviews (quarterly or per policy) using exported access data
  • Implement segregation of duties so that users who provision access are not the same users being provisioned
  • Route System Log data to a SIEM or log management platform for independent monitoring and alerting
  • Establish account lockout and password policies aligned with organizational standards

SSO and centralized credential storage

In modern deployments, SAP Convergent Mediation integrates with corporate identity providers via Single Sign-On so that authentication inherits enterprise policies: MFA, password complexity, session timeout, and conditional access. Credentials for downstream systems can be held in a cloud KMS or hyperscaler keystore rather than local configuration files. For SOC 1 purposes, this aligns platform onboarding and offboarding with the organization's IAM lifecycle and reduces the attack surface around locally stored secrets.

3. Logical Access - User Access Review

Control Objective: Periodically review all user accounts, roles, and access rights to confirm they are appropriate.

Periodic access reviews are a distinct control from day-to-day access monitoring. Auditors test whether the organization conducts structured reviews at defined intervals and whether inappropriate access is remediated.

What the platform provides

Capability

What it does

Evidence it produces

Access Controller (Users Tab)

Displays all users (local, SSO, LDAP) with username, group membership, and account status

Complete user access listing, which is exportable to a file with the System Exporter functionality resulting in a zip file

SCIM API

Enables automated retrieval of user and group configurations via REST API GET operations

Machine-readable access data for automated review workflows

  Access Controller - Users Tab showing all user accounts with username, full name, email, group membership (role), enabled status, and SSO indicator. This view provides the complete user access listing required for periodic access reviews. Data can also be exported programmatically via the SCIM REST API.

What the customer must implement

  • Schedule periodic user access reviews (typically quarterly for SOC 1)
  • Define review procedures: who reviews, what constitutes appropriate access, how exceptions are handled
  • Document review completion and any remediation actions
  • Integrate SCIM API exports into identity governance tooling where applicable

4. Processing Integrity: Counters and Reconciliation

Control Objective: Ensure that in-scope transaction data is processed completely and accurately, that errors are detected and investigated, and that records are neither lost nor duplicated between source systems and downstream financial applications.

Because mediation sits between raw operational data and the systems that drive revenue recognition, auditors will ask how you demonstrate that every in-scope record is accounted for. Process descriptions alone are not enough; auditors expect reconciled evidence.

What the platform provides

Capability

What it does

Evidence it produces

Audit Profiles

Track input files, records collected, processed, filtered, aggregated, and routed to billing or error correction, with per-workflow counters and activity reasons

Is part of the configuration needed to create audit tables in database, tracking what has been processed in detail.

 

Error Correction System (ECS)

Captures rejected records with structured error codes for investigation and reintroduction after correction

Error-code statistics and corrected-record lineage

Summary reports

Periodic roll-ups of input vs. processed vs. rejected volumes, with breakdown by error reason

The summary reports can be produced through workflow, audit profile and database tables configuration. This is optional and up to the implementor, and is recommended practice to do wherever possible.

   Audit trail showing per-file processing activity. Each row records source file, record type, record count, and activity reason (collected, aggregated, routed to billing, sent to error correction, filtered as non-billable or duplicate). This level of detail reconciles what entered the platform against what reached downstream billing.

   Monthly summary report breaking down total records in and out of the system by activity and error code. Reports like this let Revenue Assurance monitor completeness trends, investigate spikes in specific error codes, and produce reconciliation evidence for the SOC 1 control owner.

Example: daily reconciliation

A typical high-volume deployment ingests millions of usage records per day, processes them through rating and account-assignment workflows, and forwards rated usage to downstream charging and invoicing systems. Counters expose the gap between input and output: for example, 10,000,000 records ingested, 9,995,000 successfully processed, 5,000 rejected with specific error codes (unknown subscriber, invalid product mapping, duplicate). Recoverable rejections are corrected and reprocessed; the monthly reconciliation pack becomes the audit evidence.

What the customer must implement

  • Define in-scope data flows and establish reconciliation thresholds aligned with materiality
  • Assign a Revenue Assurance or equivalent function to investigate anomalies and error-code spikes
  • Retain reconciliation packs and investigation notes as audit evidence for the SOC 1 period
  • Integrate processing-chain alerts with the organization's incident response process

5. Configuration Hardening: The Property-Level SOC 1 Mapping

Beyond the three core control areas above, SAP Convergent Mediation provides something uncommon for enterprise software: a property-level SOC 1 relevance mapping across the platform's entire configuration surface.

Every configurable property has been assessed and tagged with:

  • SOC 1 Scope: Whether the property is relevant to SOC 1 compliance (Yes / No / Depends)
  • Category: The control objective it supports (Confidentiality, Integrity, or Availability)
  • Reason: A specific explanation of how misconfiguration could impact the control environment

 What this means in practice

 

Property

Category

Risk if misconfigured

mz.httpd.security

Confidentiality

Disabling TLS exposes sensitive data in transit

pico.log.level

Integrity

Insufficient logging undermines audit evidence completeness

mz.ha.enabled

Availability

Disabling HA monitoring impacts failover and uptime. However, this is just a toggle to enable the configuration and CM would rely on an external cluster software to setup HA

mz.diameter.tls.accept_all

Confidentiality

Accepting untrusted certificates enables unauthorized connections

mz.webserver.strict.host.validation

Integrity

Without host header validation, the system is exposed to DNS rebinding attacks

ec.webserver.password

Confidentiality

Default credentials risk unauthorized REST API access

The full property mapping spans Cell, Container, Execution Context, Platform, High Availability, Log, Database, and Desktop property categories.

For full properties mapping see the following DigitalRoute InfoZone: here 

This level of detail enables customers to:

  1. Baseline their configuration against SOC 1 expectations before an audit
  2. Prioritize hardening by focusing on properties tagged as SOC 1 relevant
  3. Provide evidence that configuration choices were deliberate and risk-informed

Evidence Flow: From Platform to Audit

The following diagram shows how evidence generated by SAP Convergent Mediation flows into a customer's SOC 1 control framework.

 Complementary User Entity Controls (CUECs)

When a product vendor does not operate the environment, auditors expect a clear statement of Complementary User Entity Controls, meaning controls that the customer must implement for the overall control environment to be effective.

CUEC

ITGC Domain

Description

Change approval workflow

Change Management

Operate a formal change approval process outside the platform. All configuration changes must be authorized before implementation.

Change reconciliation

Change Management

Periodically reconcile platform change logs against approved change records to detect unauthorized changes.

Access provisioning process

Logical Access

Implement formal request and approval workflows for granting, modifying, and revoking platform access.

Periodic access review

Logical Access

Conduct structured reviews of user accounts, roles, and permissions at defined intervals (minimum quarterly).

Segregation of duties

Logical Access

Ensure separation between users who provision access, approve changes, and operate the platform.

Log monitoring and alerting

IT Operations

Forward platform logs to a SIEM or equivalent. Establish alerting for anomalous administrative activity.

Reconciliation and error review

IT Operations

Operate a reconciliation process using audit trail counters and summary reports. Investigate error-code spikes and retain reconciliation packs as SOC 1 evidence.

Configuration hardening

IT Operations

Review and harden platform properties against the SOC 1 property mapping. Document configuration decisions.

Backup and recovery

IT Operations

Implement backup, retention, and disaster recovery procedures for the platform and its data stores.

Incident response

IT Operations

Include the mediation platform in the organization's incident response and escalation procedures.

Getting Started

For organizations beginning their SOC 1 alignment with SAP Convergent Mediation:

  1. Review the shared responsibility model. Understand which controls the platform supports versus which your organization must own.
  2. Map your control objectives. Align the three capability areas (change management, sensitive access monitoring, user access review) to your SOC 1 control matrix.
  3. Harden configuration. Use the property-level SOC 1 mapping to baseline and harden your deployment.
  4. Implement Complimentary User Entity Controls. Ensure all complementary controls are in place before the audit period begins.
  5. Establish evidence collection. Configure log forwarding, schedule access exports, and integrate with your ITSM and IGA platforms.

References

The following documentation provides detailed guidance for each area covered in this post:

  • SOC 1 and SOX Guidance (Overview)
     https://infozone.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/MD95/pages/751698275/SOC+1+and+SOX+Guidance
  • Change Management
     https://infozone.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/MD95/pages/751927563/Change+Management
  • Sensitive Access Monitoring
     https://infozone.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/MD95/pages/751403411/Sensitive+Access+Monitoring
  • User Access Export
     https://infozone.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/MD95/pages/751632649/User+Access+Export
  • Properties - SOC 1 Mapping Overview
     https://infozone.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/MD95/pages/752615463/Properties
  • System Properties
     https://infozone.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/MD95/pages/575407535/System+Properties
  • Platform Properties
     https://infozone.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/MD95/pages/574825573/Platform+Properties
  • Summary
     https://infozone.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/MD95/pages/752026116/Summary

SAP Convergent Mediation by DigitalRoute provides the capabilities. Your organization provides governance. Together, they form a control environment that auditors can assess with confidence.

entry.title: Connections in PaPM Cloud Universal Model
entry.content:
Hello everyone!

Setting up connections in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Universal Model (SAP PaPM Cloud UM) is straightforward (PaPM Cloud UM Connections) but there are a few important aspects -such as synchronization, mapping, and transports -that are worth understanding to get the most out of it.

In this post, you’ll learn:

  • What is a Connection
  • System Set Up: Creating a Connection
  • The difference between AddOn Environment and Environment connections
  • Transport Best Practices (Dev -> Prod)
  • Upcoming new functionality

What is a Connection?

In SAP PaPM Cloud UM, a connection acts as a bridge between a source environment and a target. It creates a logical mapping that allows you to:

Integrate model entities:

  • Consume external data
  • Reuse models across environments

Connections are environment-specific and are included in transports.

Connections in SAP PaPM Cloud UM can be grouped into three categories:

  1. Internal Connections (Environment-to-Environment)
    1. Environment (Environment Connection) Integrates Model Entities with API State = Active from one or more target environments into the source environment. 
    2. AddOn Environment (AddOn Environment Connection) Fully integrates all Model Entities with API State = Active from the target environment. Adopts target data controls and master data associations seamlessly.
  1. External Data Connections
    1. HANA Schema (HANA Schema Connection) Consumes data from an underlying data source via a specified SAP HANA schema. 
    2. OData Service (OData Service Connection) Integrates an external OData service.
    3. SAC Model (SAC Model Connection) Reads from and writes to SAC via supported APIs for data export and import. 
    4. SOAP Service (SOAP Service Connection) Connects to applications via SOAP APIs to send and write bulk data.
  1. Advanced / Specialized
    1. AI Core Service (AI Core Service Connection) Enables the consumption of AI capabilities within the environment.

System Set Up: Creating a Connection

To create a new connection, you need both a source environment and a target. The target depends on the intended use of the connection—it can either be another SAP PaPM Cloud UM environment (using an Environment or AddOn Environment connection) or an external source such as a HANA schema, OData service, SAC model, or SOAP service.

In this blog post, we will focus on connections between PaPM Cloud UM environments. To see how this works in practice, let’s walk through the steps to create and configure a connection.

  1. To create a connection, start by navigating to the Environment Overview screen. On the right-hand side, you will find the Connections section, which displays the total number of existing connections in the environment (or 0 if none exist). 
  2. Click the connection count to open the Manage Connections screen and then choose Create. A dialog window appears, where you can select the desired connection type from the dropdown list before proceeding. 
  3. Next, initiate the synchronization by clicking Sync Connection to retrieve all Model Entities with API State = Active. After that, select Sync Models to map the fields from the target to the source environment. 
  4. If adjustments are required, you can rename fields by selecting the relevant Model Entity, choosing Edit, and updating the field name accordingly before saving. Note that this option is only available for the Environment connection type. 

AddOn Environment vs. Environment Connection Type

AddOn Environment and Environment connection type are both connections to other PaPM Cloud UM Environments, but they behave differently.

The AddOn Environment connection is designed for strict consistency. It synchronizes all Model Entities with API State = Active, including their fields, associations, and data controls. The structure from the target environment is preserved as-is, meaning that no changes to field names or functions are allowed. This makes it the preferred option when you want to ensure a consistent and centrally governed data model across environments.  

In contrast, the offers more flexibility. While it also retrieves all active Model Entities and their fields, it allows adjustments to field mappings and even renaming of fields in the source environment. However, associations are not supported - only the corresponding ID fields are available. This makes the Environment connection suitable for scenarios where the source and target environments do not follow an identical data model and require adaptation.

In summary, the AddOn Environment connection prioritizes strict consistency by preserving the exact structure and relationships of the target environment, making it ideal for centrally governed and uniform data models. The Environment connection, on the other hand, provides greater flexibility by allowing field adjustments and adaptations, making it better suited for scenarios where differences between source and target data models need to be accommodated.

Transport Best Practices (Dev -> Prod)

When working across multiple tenants, following a few best practices helps ensure smooth transports and avoids common inconsistencies.

Single Source of Truth:

  • Background: Each field in PaPM Cloud UM has a hidden technical unique key used for identification. Even if field names match, keys may differ across tenants.
  • Recommendation:
    1. Perform all connection adjustments, mappings, and synchronizations in the Dev tenant.
    2. Transport both source and target environments to Prod.
    3. Do not re-synchronize Prod. Activate directly in Prod to avoid remapping which could create mismatched technical keys.
  • Why this matters:
    • Re-synchronizing in Prod may create fields with the same name but different technical keys which will cause activation errors in future transports.

Deleting a target field used by a connection (two-tenant scenario):

  • Background: You’ve completed Dev setup, transported to Prod, and activated successfully. Later, you decide to remove a field in the target environment that is used by a connected Model Entity.
  • Recommendation:
    1. Delete the field in the Dev tenant.
    2. Re-synchronize the connection in Dev and activate successfully.
    3. Transport the change (including the deletion) to Prod.
    4. Perform future changes as needed.
  • Why this matters:
    • If a similar field is later recreated in Dev with the same name, it will receive a new technical key. If the deletion was not transported to Prod, transporting the newly created field will result in two fields with the same name but different technical keys, leading to activation errors.

Upcoming new functionality

Environment connections soon support Model Views with API State = Active. It will mirror the Model Entities behavior. The Mapping of the fields from the Model View can be adjusted, and it is usable like a connected Model Entity.

I hope this overview gives you a clearer understanding of how connections work in SAP PaPM Cloud Universal Model, especially when working across multiple environments and tenants. From experience, paying attention to synchronization and transport handling early on can save quite a bit of effort later.

entry.title: Re: Handling different accrual recalculation scenarios on Leave of Absence in Employee Central
entry.content:
Hello, great input. But we face at the moment a problem with the case 2: 

We use in the rule 1. "Calculate Average Value For Numeric Job Info Field Based On Months()" and for all other case in else section: also 2 "Calculate Average Value For Numeric Job Info Field()"

With the Numeric Job info field we have the consider eligibility button and it works just fine. But if user started on 1.1. and also Parental leave ends for example on the 31.3 it uses the first block and Calculate Average Value For Numeric Job Info Field Based On Months(). In this case the calculation is wrong  because we do not have a "consider eligibility" button So average working days gets calculated for 12 months and not for only the 9 months (1.4 till 31.12) where the users is active.  I need to manipulate the job info date for calculation to be correct (change working days during parental leave to get the correct average value). Can you suggest any other workaround?

entry.title: Re: Pssst! Want to learn a Datasphere secret?
entry.content:
Thank you @CLTGravesen for bringing this up! There is so much to write about DSP and SAC 🙂 Using the session context for a text view is the most consistent and future proof way of modeling.

S4 Extraction CDS Views often (or always?) inherit the text column in the attribute table, when the text is not language dependent. Maybe this is why DSP works that way? We will probably never know...

entry.title: Regarding SAP GRC
entry.content:
Hey everyone,

I hope you are all doing well.

I am writing to seek guidance on how to build a career in SAP Governance, Risk, and Compliance (SAP GRC).

I recently completed my Bachelor's degree in Computer Science and Engineering with a specialization in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning. I am very interested in developing expertise in SAP GRC and would like to understand the best path to become job-ready in this domain.

I would greatly appreciate your advice on the following:

  1. Recommended courses or certifications for SAP GRC.
  2. The key modules and topics I should focus on.
  3. Suggested hands-on practice or training resources.
  4. Career opportunities and entry-level roles related to SAP GRC.
  5. Any additional details that I should know about this domain

My goal is to gain the necessary knowledge and practical skills to pursue entry-level opportunities in SAP GRC and related risk and compliance roles.

entry.title: 3 Common Mistakes I See in SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding Implementations
entry.content:
3 Common Mistakes I See in SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding Implementations


In my first blog post, I wrote about how onboarding represents one of the most critical "moments of truth" in the employee journey, and how SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding can help organizations deliver a structured, consistent, and scalable experience.

This is the next installment of blog posts.

Having been part of more than 20 implementations across industries, I have also seen the same mistakes - or challenges - appear repeatedly. That is not a criticism. It is hard to know what you need before you have lived with the system, and SAP SuccessFactors moves fast. What was a limitation two releases ago may be a solved problem today.

These are the three I see most often.

Mistake 1: Overdesigning upfront and underinvesting in iteration

Customers want to get it right the first time. They invest heavily in the design phase, trying to anticipate every scenario and map out the perfect process before go-live. The ambition is really good. The problem is that it is genuinely hard to know how well an onboarding process is designed before it meets reality. Real new hires, real hiring managers, and real edge cases have a way of surfacing things that no workshop ever could.

The good news is that the system is built for iteration. Adjusting task sequences, updating notification content, tweaking email timing, refining what gets triggered for which population: none of this requires a project. It requires a decision and an afternoon.

My recommendation is to go live with something solid, observe what actually happens, and then revisit the configuration after a few months with real data behind you.

That data is available. Story Reporting in SAP SuccessFactors gives you clear insight into the onboarding process: are new hires completing tasks on time, where are the bottlenecks, which steps have a tendency to get stuck. These are not questions you can answer before go-live. But once you can, they give you really good indications on where to focus.

It is also often at this stage that the need for multiple process variants or onboarding programs becomes visible. What looked like one onboarding population turns out to be two or three, each with meaningfully different needs.
That is not a failure of the initial design. It is the natural outcome of running the process in the real world, and paying attention to what it reveals.

Mistake 2: Not using Onboarding Custom Tasks

The standard task types in SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding are solid.
For many organizations, they cover the majority of what is needed, and there is nothing wrong with building your onboarding experience primarily on standard functionality. Trying to customize everything is its own mistake.

But there are situations where the standard does not stretch far enough. When that happens, the right answer is not to twist the standard into something it was not designed to do. It is to use the framework SAP has built specifically for this purpose.

Custom Onboarding Tasks allow organizations to build tasks that are specific to a role, a location, a business unit, or a phase of onboarding. A new hire in a warehouse operation has entirely different day-one needs than someone joining a corporate finance team. Custom Tasks allow you to reflect that reality inside the system. And it is worth saying: this is not building from scratch. The framework is there. What you build within it is largely a question of creativity.

The reason customers avoid this is usually apprehension around working with custom MDF objects, or a lingering perception that custom tasks are unreliable. That perception may have had some basis a few years ago. SAP has invested significantly in this area since, and the stability and flexibility of custom tasks has improved considerably.

The 1H 2026 release makes this even more accessible. SAP has introduced pre-delivered custom task template objects that serve as a curated starting point for configuration, specifically designed to reduce the manual setup burden and lower the barrier to entry. The blank-page problem that made custom tasks feel daunting is no longer the obstacle it once was.

Mistake 3: Treating onboarding as the last step instead of a trigger

The standard notification setup in SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding is already quite capable. Out of the box, the system sends well-timed, relevant communications to new hires, hiring managers, HR, and other onboarding participants. For many organizations, this covers most of what is needed.

But onboarding does not exist in isolation. It sits alongside IT provisioning, facilities management, payroll setup, and a range of other processes owned by different teams, often in entirely separate systems. The question is how those systems find out what they need to know, and when.

This is where most implementations leave value on the table. The conversation usually stops at "we have notifications configured" without asking what those notifications could actually connect to. Email notifications to the right external recipients are a starting point, but SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding also exposes APIs and status events that allow external systems to actively consume onboarding data and act on it in real time. That opens up a different class of integration entirely: one where downstream systems do not wait for an email to arrive, but respond directly to what is happening in the onboarding process.

In a landscape where most organizations are already running automation platforms, integration middleware, or AI-driven service management tools, this capability is worth taking seriously. The building blocks are there in the platform. Whether they get used depends on whether anyone asked the question during the implementation.

Closing thoughts

Onboarding is never really finished, and that is not a problem. It is an opportunity!
Whether you are scoping your first implementation, preparing to go live, or running a process that has been in place for years, there is almost always something worth revisiting.
The platform keeps moving. Your configuration should too.

Jonas Kyhnau is a SAP SuccessFactors Senior Consultant at Pentos Denmark. I help organizations optimize and streamline their core HR processes with practical, scalable solutions, covering Recruiting (including SmartRecruiters), Onboarding, Time Management and Employee Central.

entry.title: Re: #CoE #DH01 Muti-Bank Connectivity with S/4HANA Cloud...
entry.content:
Hi,

 

Just to clarify the behavior in SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity scenarios.
 
For bank statement processing, technical status and error details are primarily available in the “Manage Bank Messages” app. This app provides message-level logs, processing status, and payload information for both inbound and outbound messages.
 
The “Manage Bank Statements” app in SAP S/4HANA is used at the business level, after the bank statement has been successfully processed and made available for posting.

 

So in practice:
- Use “Manage Bank Messages” → for technical monitoring and troubleshooting 
- Use “Manage Bank Statements” → for reviewing and posting bank statements 

 

Further information:
SAP Help Portal – SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity 
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_MULTI_BANK_CONNECTIVITY

 

Regards 
Mahendra C
MBC Team

entry.title: Re: #CoE #DH01 Process Flow for Multi-Bank Connectivity (...
entry.content:
Hello


In SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity (MBC), the standard process flow (such as Best Practice 16R) primarily covers payment files (outbound) and bank statement processing (inbound).

If your requirement is to retrieve only bank account balances (without full transaction statements):

- Bank account balance information is typically provided as part of bank statement messages (for example CAMT.052 / CAMT.053 formats)
- MBC acts as a transmission layer and forwards these messages to SAP S/4HANA
- The balance is then used in SAP S/4HANA for cash positioning and reporting purposes

There is no separate native MBC process that pulls only balances independently of bank statement messages. Balance updates are generally derived from the incoming statement files rather than a standalone balance-only integration.

Further information:
SAP Help Portal – SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity 
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_MULTI_BANK_CONNECTIVITY

Regards 
Mahendra

entry.title: Re: #CoE #DH01 Muti-Bank Connectivity with S/4HANA Cloud
entry.content:
Hi,
 
In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, integration with SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity (MBC) is supported through predefined scope items and standard configuration.

 

For setup on the S/4HANA Cloud side, the key aspects are:

 

- Activate the relevant scope item:
  - 16R – Bank Communication Management with SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity

 

- Maintain bank master data and house bank configuration

 

- Configure sender and receiver IDs (used for routing messages via MBC)
- Set up communication arrangements and connectivity for MBC integration
 
Once these prerequisites are in place, MBC acts as a middleware to transmit payment files and bank statements between SAP S/4HANA Cloud and the bank.

 

Further information:
SAP Help Portal – SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity 
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_MULTI_BANK_CONNECTIVITY 

 

 

Regards 
Mahendra C
MBC Team

entry.title: Re: BCM Outgoing File
entry.content:
Hi,
 
For BCM scenarios integrated with SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity (MBC), outbound file handling differs from traditional FTP or PI/PO-based setups.
 
1) File transfer to bank:
SAP does not place files directly on bank FTP in MBC scenarios. Instead:
- The payment file is sent from SAP to MBC via API
- MBC then handles transmission to the bank (via SFTP, EBICS, or other supported channels)

 

So no local FTP/path configuration is required in SAP when MBC is used.

 

2) Approval process in BCM:
Approval workflows in BCM are typically configured based on payment items or predefined rules. Approval logic (per transaction vs batch level) is controlled in BCM workflow configuration.

 

Batch-level approval (for example, based on total payment file value) is possible depending on workflow rule design and configuration, but requires proper setup in BCM approval rules.

 

Further information:
SAP Help Portal – SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity 
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_MULTI_BANK_CONNECTIVITY

entry.title: Re: How to create bank interfaces
entry.content:
Hi,
 
In SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity scenarios, bank interfaces are not created using traditional T‑Codes or SPRO paths like in classic SAP ERP setups.

 

Instead, connectivity is established through standard integration frameworks:

 

- SAP S/4HANA or ERP sends payment and bank communication messages to MBC via APIs
- MBC acts as middleware and handles communication with banks using supported protocols such as SFTP, EBICS, or SWIFT

 

On the ERP side, the key setup typically involves:
- Bank master data and house bank configuration
- Sender/Receiver ID mapping (used for routing)
- Communication arrangements for MBC connectivity

 

There is no single “bank interface T‑Code” in MBC-based architectures, as the integration is based on standardized connectivity rather than custom file interfaces.

 

Further information:
SAP Help Portal – SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity 
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_MULTI_BANK_CONNECTIVITY

 

Regards 
Mahendra C
MBC Team

entry.title: Re: SAP DMEE File Generation having last 10 digits accoun...
entry.content:
Hi,
 
The behavior you described is related to ERP configuration (account number length and DMEE structure), and SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity does not modify or validate account numbers within payment files.
 
MBC simply transmits the file generated by SAP to the bank without changing its content. Therefore, the correctness and completeness of the account number must be ensured during file generation on the ERP side.

 

Providing only part of the account number (such as the last 10 digits) may lead to rejection by the bank, depending on the bank’s requirements and validation rules.

 

It is recommended to ensure that the full account number is correctly maintained and supported in the ERP configuration and that the generated payment file aligns with the bank’s expected format.

 

Further information:
SAP Help Portal – SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity 
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_MULTI_BANK_CONNECTIVITY

 

Regards 
Mahendra C
MBC Team

entry.title: Re: FSN - How to handle non member financial institutions...
entry.content:
Hi,

 

In SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity (MBC) / Financial Services Network (FSN) scenarios, integration with non‑member banks (i.e., banks not part of the FSN network) is still supported.

 

For such cases:
- Non‑member banks are typically connected using supported communication channels like SFTP, EBICS, or SWIFT
- MBC acts as a middleware and can route messages to these banks even if they are not part of the FSN/member bank network
- Additional configuration such as endpoint setup, file formats, and authentication details must be agreed with the bank

 

So, while member banks allow plug‑and‑play integration, non‑member banks require custom connectivity setup via supported protocols.

 

Further information:
SAP Help Portal – SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity 
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_MULTI_BANK_CONNECTIVITY

 

Regards 
Mahendra C
MBC Team
